sentences.text: I am sorry, but I forgot it.
I want to mention a very good friend of mine.
His name was Eugan Peisak, P-E-I-S-S-A-K.
He lived in the house of my grandmother, because the Jews had to move close together at the time, because there was already destruction of homes.
So they moved people together.
The Jews had to move together closer, even.
So he lived in the house with my grandmother and myself.
And he-- one day he said, I'm going to go for a walk.
I go to the American embassy.
I'm going to get a waiting number, a [GERMAN].
I said, what is this?
He said, do you want to come along?
I said, well, what is a [GERMAN]?
I didn't even know what he was talking about.
It was 1939.
And he said, come along.
It doesn't cost you anything.
I mean, I get one too.
So I went up there.
That's how I got my waiting number.
That's how I got that 20,687.
Otherwise, I don't think I'd ever would have gotten out of Spain.
I don't know what would have happened to me.
He never got out.
He never got out.
He never got out.
I don't know what happened to him.
But I checked the files in Auschwitz, he is not there.
But he was killed.
He got married just shortly before I left, and he was killed with his wife.
That's right, from Barcelona.
Otherwise, I would have been sitting there with my-- with my visa for Liberia, and I never could have landed there.
They wouldn't have taken me on the boat, I'm sure, because they knew all the rules.
I was in the US.
I-- the next day, in fact, I stayed with a cousin of mine, once removed, with my mother.
He lived in Long Island, in Woodmere.

And I worked in his business-- the next day, he took me right to his business and on the Long Island Railroad, and then the subway in Pennsylvania Station, and then going to-- on the subway to his business.
And there I worked as a stock clerk.
I took the packages to the post office with the little push cart.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after about-- I did that for about two weeks.
He told me he was going on a vacation.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt-- I mean, I would have stayed there all by myself at the house, you know.
And my English wasn't too good.
I spoke English because I had learned English before, but it wasn't too good.
And I just felt I had an inkling that he wasn't too keen that I would stay at the house.
So he said, maybe you want to look around, you know, for a room or some place to live, maybe with a friend in Manhattan.
I must say that I made only $8.40 a week at the time, so I couldn't get very far.
So the day before he left on vacation, I said also goodbye to him.
And I went with my two suitcases.
I went to Manhattan.
And took the suitcases first to the place where I worked, and then after hours-- at the time you still worked 48 hours-- and then after hours, I went with my suitcase.
I had called a friend of mine who had given-- who was one of the two who had given the $120 for my passage.
And he said on the telephone, he said, take the IRT train, you know, and get off at 72nd Street, and look for rooming house.
You know, they say 'room to let.'
So I-- it was not easy to get on the subway with two suitcases.
You know, they had these turn-- what do you call it, where you get in and out, you know, where you drop your nickle?
At the time, you dropped a nickle.
That was the fare.
And I got off at 72nd street.
I found there were plenty of rooms to let.
And I got one that cost me $3.50 a week.
Walk up four flights, you know.
And it was under the roof, you know, where the roof goes this way.
It was very hot in summer.
I had to open that little roof window, you know, with that-- with a-- with that hook so it stayed open.
And, well, there's also a little story, if I may.
My English wasn't too good.
And there were two girls who were living on the same floor, so they were rooming with each other, staying in the same room.
And they said, what time do you have to leave?
I told them my time so we could coordinate our time to go to the bathroom, because the rooms didn't have a bathroom.
It was a bathroom on the floor, you know?
So I was the first one to go to the bathroom in the morning.
And they said they would have to come in at certain time.
So the bathroom had only a little hook, you know?
And I put that hook on from the inside-- I thought I had put it on, but apparently I hadn't.
I was shaving, and the door opened, you know?
And I said, oh!
And then she said, oh, I'm sorry, You?
Know, she said.
And I said, oh, you are welcome.
[LAUGHS] Just learning English.
My English.
Yes.
Well, I was corresponding with by parents, and with my grandmother, and with my aunt.
This was correspondence with Czechoslovakia, with [NON-ENGLISH] and correspondence with Stuttgart.
And I was writing about once a week.
And they were writing to me, I would say more often.
But I was dead tired, I was dead tired.
And the postage was three cents at the time, if I remember correctly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no air mail.
It was all ship mail.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still have their letters.
I kept every one of them.
And, in fact, in some of the letters my brother still wrote a little hello, you know, in some of the letters.
My brother spent four years at concentration camps-- almost four years.
And then I got another job.
I got many jobs.
If someone offered me $0.50 more per week, I changed jobs, you know?
It wasn't easy to get jobs at the time because there was plenty of unemployment.
And then I finally got a job, after about three or four different ones.
I got a job as a tool and dye maker.
I never was a tool and dye maker, but they were looking for tool and dye makers all over the place.
This was in 1941, when the war had started with Japan, you know?
And they were looking for tool and dye makers.
But this was the only position you could get easily, I mean, if you were a tool and dye maker.
So I answered an ad.
I went there, and I said I was a tool and dye maker, which of course, I wasn't.
They put me on the lathe.
A broke off the tool bits, one after the other.
And after about four hours, I was out again.
So again I was looking for a job as a tool and dye maker.
And I got into a place, a company called [GERMAN].
These were Germans.
And there, I was lucky that I was being assigned to a German fellow who had come to the States in the early '30s.
And he gave me something to do, and he saw right away that I didn't have any idea whatsoever.
And he said, now, there are two possibilities, he said.
I know you told me you're a refugee.
I want to be helpful.
If I can help you, I'll be very happy to, but you have to come out clean.
So I told my whole story.
And he said, all right, I'll try to protect you as much as I can.
And he showed me.
And to make a long story short, I really became a tool and dye maker under his auspices.
He was very, very nice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, at some point you joined the US Army.
I didn't join.
I was drafted.
Yes.
I wanted to volunteer at that time because I felt, now, very strongly against the Germans.
And I wanted to-- I volunteered for the Czech Army, for the Czech Air Force.
They had a training arrangement in Canada, because they had a Czech Air Force-- what do they call it, because there was no Czech government anymore?
But there were Czechs who were being trained by the English, and by the Canadians, you know, to fight for the-- for the war effort.
And they had-- I had heard they had a training camp up there.
So I went to the Czech consulate.
And the Czech consulate told me, we are sorry, you don't speak any Czech.
You have a Czech passport, yes, but you don't speak any Czech.
For us, you are not Czech.
You are a German.
You speak German.
So that was-- that I couldn't do.
So I continued working, and then-- until one day, I got the greetings from the President of the United States.
Which meant the-- Draft.
--the draft.
And I was-- went to the Grand Central Station to get the physical examination.
I was accepted.
I was shipped to Fort Dix.
And from Fort Dix, I was shipped to my basic training to Camp Joseph T.
Robinson in Arkansas, near Little Rock-- about nine miles out of Little Rock.
There they made me a US citizen, in the District Court.
From there, after the basic training, which was three months, I was shipped to For-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fort Lee in-- near Shreveport, Louisiana.
And from there, I was shipped to my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] battle outfit, to the 204th Field Artillery Battalion, which, at the time, were on maneuvers in Tennessee.
So I joined them in-- maneuvers on maneuvers.
And we were on maneuvers for about three months.
And then we were shipped out to England.
At the time, we didn't know we were going to England.
We only knew that we were going overseas.
In fact, when we were still on the-- on the boat, we were not sure we were going to England.
We knew that we were most likely going to Europe, because we-- we were on the boat, leaving from New York.
So we thought it was going to be Europe.
But we felt it might be North Africa, or it might be Italy, or any place--
Yes.
We didn't know-- yes.
Back after about 40 months.
I was--
I was going back.
Well--
I felt very keen about it.
I mean, at that time you could have given me-- you could have sent-- sent me into a bomber, and given me bombs, and said, all right, it's a suicide mission.
Drop them over the [GERMAN], you know, which is the building where Hitler and his ministers were and go ahead and do it.
I think I would have done it.
I mean, I felt very, very strong because I had received those letters from my parents.
I had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the letters were censored.
My letters were censored.
I mean, if I could show you letters, they still have the sign of the censors-- censor number so-and-so, with the swastika on it.
They were all red, some things were cut off.
And I felt very strong about fighting the Germans now.
But I must also say that when I left Germany, the German army was at it's zenith.
They were in Africa.
They were almost in Egypt.
In Russia, they were about 100 miles from [RUSSIAN].
So they were winning.
I mean, they had many casualties, but they were winning all over, on all fronts.
Stalingrad came afterwards-- the battle for Stalingrad.
So I felt very strong about fighting the Germans.
But I was not so sure whether the Allies would win the war, at that particular moment in time.
So, we landed-- just before we landed, the loudspeaker system on the ship announced that we were landing in England-- in Liverpool, England.
That's actually when we knew where we were.
We didn't know before.
So there, again, we were trained.
We were going to the firing range, as I was in the artillery.
I was in the Fire Direction Center, where you would-- Headquarters Battery-- where you calculated the elevation of the-- of the-- pieces, we called them.
The cannons, actually, but we called them pieces-- the artillery, how they had to be adjusted, you know?
I mean, the angle.

And then we had to adjust for the-- the meteorological messages, you know, according to the direction of the wind, and the humidity, and the-- because all that influenced the direction of the flight, and you had to adjust for all that.
So I was, what they called at the time, a computer, with a slide rule, you know, in the Fire Direction Center.
And I got there, actually, in England.
Before, I was a machine gun [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] man.
And in England, again, we had maneuvers.
And then about one month before the landing-- we didn't know where the landing was, but they showed us, in miniature, where we were going to land.
There were many buildings there.
There were the roads there.
There were the hedge rows-- the famous hedge rows in France.
And it was a pretty good idea, where we were.
We didn't know where.
We only knew that it was going to be in France, but not where.
And then we started the landing maneuvers, you know?
We went on lakes.
At the very last, maybe two weeks, before we shipped across, our outfit went to Swansea.

We shipped out from Swansea, because they couldn't ship-- that huge fleet-- invasion fleet couldn't ship out from the same spot, so they shipped out from different-- different ports in south of England, you know?
Most of them shipped out of Plymouth.
But many others shipped out of other places.
We shipped out of Swansea, which was actually a summer resort for the English.
And there we had-- there we trained on-- actually, in the ocean.
There we trained how to come down from the ship into the landing barges.
And then, one day it was-- it was it.
We actually landed-- I landed D-plus-three.
That means D-day, which was June the 6th, plus three.
I landed on June the 9th.
In Normandy, yes.
We landed on-- on Omaha Beach.
It was Omaha Beach and Utah Beach.
That's where the American's landed.
And when we landed, which was three days after the invasion, it looked just terrible.
It was just awful.
I mean, we were still under German artillery fire.
We were still under German machine gun fire.
We piled the dead.
The US soldiers, they were piled three and four corpses high.
Looking for cover behind the corpses, you know?
It was just awful.
And this was the third day of the invasion.
It was-- it's the 6th, 7th, 8th-- the fourth day of the invasion, actually.
The fourth day after the invasion.
So, gradually-- I mean, of course we had big, big support.
I mean, you name it.
We had support from-- from the-- the ships.
I mean, they were-- the whole-- all the German fortifications were under fire.
The Allied-- Lots of military support.
Military support.
I mean, there were bombers.
You could only see US bombers there.
And bombs were flying right and left.
We only hoped, you know, that-- our problem was that the bombs were dropped too far in, because they were afraid to drop them too close to us.
So they were dropped pretty far in.
But later on, when we started to advance, we saw the damage those bombs had achieved.
So, well, I was with the third army-- with Patton's army.
We were the 204th Artillery Battalion, was attached to the 20th Corps.
We had the 90th Infantry Division and the 20th Corps, and other tank divisions, armored divisions, and the infantry divisions.
And before our battle inside, it took us about-- about six weeks to break out of Normandy, because that's a peninsula, you know?
We couldn't get out of that.
It was near-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] near Arromanches, where we broke out.
And there were many casualties.
And we had children prisoners.
Yeah, I was unofficially interrogating children prisoners, because they always called me, you know?
And-- Did your company know that you were a Jew?
I was in a battery.
In the field artillery, they called them Batteries.
What is a Company in the infantry, is a Battery in the artillery.
[DRINKS] No.
I must say, they took me-- they-- my commanding officer knew it, but even-- and some other knew it too.
But strangely to say, they always took me more for a German than for a Jew, yes.

This is also the reason that, as I found out later on when we had a get-together with my outfit after the war-- a few years ago we got together, a reunion, you know, of the 204th Field Artillery Battalion-- and I said, listen, I mean, I thought I was a good computer.
My commanding officer said, yes, I knew it, Richard.
But at the time, we were-- I tell you the truth is that we were never too sure of you.

We always watched over your shoulders whether your firing instructions, at least at the beginning, were correct, whether they were not too short, you know, I mean, the range, which I gave to the-- to the cannons, to the pieces, you know, the artillery pieces.
But as the-- as the war progressed, you know, they felt more sure of my person.
And I never got that feeling, but he told me that at the reunion.
I fought the war in Europe.
We crossed the Seine in Fontainebleau, south of Paris.
We got held up in Metz, again.
There was a German artillery-- German officer school.
They defended themselves to the last.
There were many, many casualties.
And then we got to the [NON-ENGLISH].
And there were-- that was in the same moment the German counteroffensive in the Ardennes.
December of 1944, it started.
And then, I got-- lo and behold, I got transferred from the field artillery.
I never made an application, but they must have seen it from my-- from my record, you know, at headquarters, or I don't know where.
I got transferred to military intelligence.
I went to the military intelligence school in [FRENCH] near Paris, where I was for about six weeks.
There, only German was spoken.
They were all German Jews, or German Gentiles, but all American soldiers.
No.
There were certain rumors.
But not as bad as they proved-- reality proved to be later on.
So we were [FRENCH].
And then I was shipped back, not to my original outfit any more, but now as a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] military intelligence [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] person with the 86th Infantry Division.
I joined-- I joined them near Cologne.
Back in Germany.
No, no.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] This was in February.
This was the end of February.
The war was over May 8th.
This was in February, so, '45.
And then we battled our way south.
And we went to-- finally, we got to Salzburg, which is in Austria, already.
And this is when a flap off a tank next to me-- I was sitting in a jeep.
It was raining, and the flap of the tank opened, and a head came out.
And he went like this, which meant stop, or over, you know.
So because, you know, when tanks move they rattle, you cannot hear.
You don't hear anything.
So he went like this.
So I stopped, and he stopped, and he said, we just got the news, you know, that the war is over.
So this was good news, of course.

Before the war was over, when I was attached to the 86th Infantry Division, my intelligence team-- we were six in the division-- we went to Berchtesgaden, to where Hitler had his vacation home, or whatever you want to call it.
OK.
